name: Apache Cassandra
description: Highly scalable, distributed wide-column NoSQL database designed for high availability with no single point of failure

license.comment: True open source under Apache License 2.0.

hosting-options.comment: Self-hosted is primary deployment. DataStax Astra is the official managed service. Also available via Instaclustr, AWS Keyspaces, and others.

initial-release.comment: Originally developed at Facebook, released as open source in July 2008, became Apache top-level project in 2010.

data-model.comment: Partitioned row store with wide-column model. Data organized into tables with rows and columns.

topology.comment: Peer-to-peer architecture where all nodes are equal. No single point of failure. Uses gossip protocol for cluster communication.

sharding.comment: Uses Murmur3 hash partitioner by default to distribute data across nodes. Partition key determines data placement.

replication-factor.comment: Configurable replication factor per keyspace. Supports different replication strategies including NetworkTopologyStrategy for multi-datacenter deployments.

multi-region.comment: Native support for multi-datacenter deployments. Can withstand entire datacenter outages with no data loss.

cap-position.comment: Defaults to AP (availability and partition tolerance). Consistency is tunable per-operation - can be configured for strong consistency with QUORUM or ALL levels.

consistency-model.comment: Tunable consistency from eventual (ONE) to strong (QUORUM) to linearizable (via lightweight transactions with Paxos).

acid-transactions.comment: No full ACID transactions. Lightweight transactions (LWT) provide linearizable consistency for single-partition compare-and-set operations using Paxos.

transaction-scope.comment: Lightweight transactions limited to single-partition operations. Batch statements provide atomicity within a single partition.

isolation-levels.comment: Traditional isolation levels don't apply. Cassandra uses tunable consistency levels (ONE, QUORUM, ALL, etc.) instead.

distributed-transactions.comment: No cross-partition distributed transactions. Lightweight transactions use Paxos but are limited to single partition.

sql-compatibility.comment: CQL (Cassandra Query Language) is SQL-like but not SQL-compatible. No JOINs, subqueries, or aggregations across partitions.

secondary-indexes.comment: Storage-Attached Indexing (SAI) in Cassandra 5.0 provides efficient secondary indexes. Previous versions had 2i and SASI.

joins.comment: No JOIN support. Data model should be denormalized for query patterns.

aggregations.comment: Basic aggregations (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX) supported within a single partition. User-defined aggregates available.

full-text-search.comment: No built-in full-text search. SAI supports LIKE queries and some text searching. External solutions like Elasticsearch often used.

max-cluster-size.comment: Apple runs over 160,000 Cassandra instances storing 100+ petabytes across 1,000+ clusters.

write-throughput.comment: Apple regularly achieves millions of operations per second. Netflix handles over 1 trillion requests per day.

read-latency-p99.comment: Low latency optimized. Actual latency depends on consistency level, cluster configuration, and data model.

auto-scaling.comment: Nodes can be added without downtime, but scaling is a manual operation. Some managed services offer auto-scaling.

hot-spot-handling.comment: Hot spots must be addressed through data model design. Proper partition key selection is critical.

zero-downtime-upgrades.comment: Rolling upgrades supported. Nodes upgraded one at a time while cluster remains available.

online-schema-changes.comment: Schema changes propagate via gossip protocol. Most schema changes don't require downtime.

point-in-time-recovery.comment: Possible via commitlog archiving combined with snapshots, but not built-in. Requires external tooling or managed service features.

backup-strategy.comment: Built-in snapshot and incremental backup via SSTable hard links. Point-in-time recovery requires commitlog archiving.

kubernetes-operator.comment: K8ssandra cass-operator is the community-standard Kubernetes operator, originally from DataStax. Supports automated deployment, scaling, and operations.

go-driver.comment: GoCQL is the well-established community driver, linked from official docs.

java-driver.comment: Official Apache Cassandra Java driver (formerly DataStax driver), now maintained by Apache as of version 4.18.

dotnet-driver.comment: DataStax C# driver available, community maintained.

python-driver.comment: DataStax Python driver (cassandra-driver package) is the official driver.

orm-support.comment: Limited ORM support due to wide-column data model. Spring Data Cassandra available for Java.

open-source.comment: True open source under Apache License 2.0. Apache Software Foundation project.

free-tier.comment: DataStax Astra offers free tier with $25/month credit (80GB storage, 20M ops/month).

pricing-model.comment: Astra uses per-operation and per-storage pricing. Self-hosted has no licensing cost. Enterprise support available.

cost-estimate-small.comment: Varies by usage pattern. Astra pay-as-you-go pricing. Self-hosted only costs infrastructure.

github-stars.comment: As of December 2024. Repository has 9.5k stars and 3.8k forks.

stackoverflow-tags.comment: Over 20,000 questions tagged with 'cassandra' on Stack Overflow.

change-data-capture.comment: Built-in CDC since Cassandra 3.8. Captures changes via commitlog hard links. Works with Debezium for Kafka integration.

watch-subscribe.comment: No native watch/subscribe mechanism for real-time push notifications on data changes. Users must implement solutions using CDC with Kafka or custom event-driven architectures.

triggers.comment: Supports triggers via custom Java code. Trigger logic must be written in JVM languages, compiled to JAR, and deployed to lib/triggers on every node. Fires before DML operations.

event-streaming.comment: Kafka integration via Debezium Cassandra connector is the primary event streaming solution. CEP-44 proposes native Kafka integration via Sidecar. No built-in Pulsar or Kinesis connectors from vendor.
